commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Human review should accept or reject the fail-closed compatibility tradeoff; there is no narrow automated repair to request.; Cleared: The diff does not add dependency, workflow, credential, or supply-chain exposure and instead tightens a remote media availability boundary. (sha=9819a5e8e5990106405e49a7cbcd1e169105e413)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a streamed byte cap to podcast remote-media temp-file downloads, documents the 512 MB remote podcast/media limit, and adds regression tests for streaming and non-streaming oversized downloads.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main can reach downloadToFile after missing or under-reported HEAD metadata, and downloadToFile writes streamed chunks without a max byte guard.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human review should accept or reject the fail-closed compatibility tradeoff; there is no narrow automated repair to request.

target.title: [security] fix(podcast): cap remote media file downloads
target.body:
## Summary

This PR hardens remote podcast/media transcription downloads so the documented remote media size limit is enforced during the actual GET stream, not only during the optional HEAD preflight.

- Enforces `MAX_REMOTE_MEDIA_BYTES` while writing large remote media to a temp file.
- Rejects responses that omit, fail, or under-report `Content-Length` once the streamed bytes exceed the cap.
- Covers both streaming responses and the non-streaming `arrayBuffer()` fallback with regression tests.

## Security issues covered

| Issue | Impact | Severity |
|-------|--------|----------|
| Remote media temp-file downloads trusted HEAD metadata but did not enforce a byte cap during GET streaming | A malicious podcast/feed media URL could cause the CLI to keep writing an oversized response to local temp storage, leading to disk/resource exhaustion | Medium |

## Before this PR

- `transcribeMediaUrl()` rejected media only when a successful HEAD response reported `content-length > MAX_REMOTE_MEDIA_BYTES`.
- If HEAD failed, omitted `Content-Length`, or reported a smaller value than the GET body, the ffmpeg temp-file path streamed the full response to `/tmp/summarize-podcast-*.bin`.
- The temp-file downloader had no byte ceiling of its own.

## After this PR

- The ffmpeg temp-file path passes `MAX_REMOTE_MEDIA_BYTES` into `downloadToFile()`.
- `downloadToFile()` checks the next byte count before every file write and throws `Remote media too large` instead of writing beyond the configured cap.
- The non-streaming `arrayBuffer()` fallback also rejects oversized responses before writing a file.
- Regression tests cover both paths.
- Documentation now states that remote podcast/media transcription downloads are capped at 512 MB and fail closed with `Remote media too large` when `Content-Length` is missing or dishonest.

## Why this matters

Remote media URLs can be controlled by a feed publisher or by whoever supplies a media URL to the CLI. A size check that only trusts HEAD metadata is not enough because servers can omit HEAD support, omit `Content-Length`, use chunked transfer, or advertise a smaller size than the body returned by GET.

## Attack flow

```text
malicious podcast/feed media URL
    -> podcast/media transcription temp-file path
        -> HEAD missing/under-reports size
            -> GET stream writes without a hard byte cap
                -> local temp disk/resource exhaustion
```

## Affected code

| Issue | Files |
|-------|-------|
| Remote media temp-file download cap | `packages/core/src/content/transcript/providers/podcast/media.ts`, `tests/security.remote-media-file-download-cap.test.ts` |

## Root cause

Remote media temp-file download cap:
- The preflight size check was metadata-only and did not enforce the same limit at the streaming sink.
- The trusted boundary was the actual bytes written to local temp storage, but the guard lived only before the GET response body was consumed.

## CVSS assessment

| Issue | CVSS v3.1 | Vector |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| Remote media temp-file download cap | 5.3 Medium | `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:L` |

Rationale:
- The attacker can host or supply a remote media URL, but a user/agent still has to summarize or transcribe that URL.
- The demonstrated impact is local availability/resource exhaustion, not code execution or data disclosure.

## Safe reproduction steps

1. Use a fetch stub whose HEAD metadata reports a bounded size, but whose GET stream returns one extra byte beyond the configured download cap.
2. Call `downloadToFile()` with a small test `maxBytes` value.
3. Before this PR, the promise resolves and writes all bytes returned by GET.
4. After this PR, the promise rejects with `Remote media too large` before writing past the cap.

The new regression test uses the same pattern safely with local in-memory streams and a temporary file.

## Real behavior proof

I also ran a real HTTP download proof (not a fetch stub) against a local loopback server that omitted `Content-Length` and streamed one byte-range beyond the production `MAX_REMOTE_MEDIA_BYTES` cap. The URL/port are redacted because the server was local and ephemeral.

Command:

```sh
pnpm -s tsx /tmp/summarize-remote-media-cap-proof.ts
```

Redacted terminal output:

```text
proof: real HTTP oversized remote media download
url: http://127.0.0.1:<redacted-port>/redacted-oversized-episode.mp3
content-length: omitted
configured_cap_bytes: 536870912
server_stream_bytes: 537919488
result: Remote media too large (512MB). Limit is 512MB.
get_requests: 1
bytes_sent_before_rejection: 537919488
```

This exercises the actual HTTP GET streaming path and shows the remote media URL failing closed with `Remote media too large` once the 512 MB cap is exceeded, even without `Content-Length`.

## Expected vulnerable behavior

- Pre-patch streaming download result: `downloadToFile()` resolved after writing `maxBytes + 1` bytes.
- Pre-patch non-streaming fallback result: `downloadToFile()` resolved after writing `maxBytes + 1` bytes.
- Post-patch behavior: both paths reject with `Remote media too large`.

## Changes in this PR

- Adds an optional `maxBytes` guard to `downloadToFile()`.
- Passes `MAX_REMOTE_MEDIA_BYTES` from the podcast/media transcription temp-file path.
- Rejects oversized non-streaming responses before writing them to disk.
- Reuses a single remote-media-too-large error helper for HEAD and GET enforcement.
- Adds regression tests for streaming and non-streaming oversized downloads.
- Updates `docs/media.md` so maintainers/users can deliberately accept the enforced 512 MB remote podcast/media cap.

## Files changed

| Category | Files | What changed |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| Runtime guard | `packages/core/src/content/transcript/providers/podcast/media.ts` | Enforces the remote media byte limit at the temp-file write sink |
| Tests | `tests/security.remote-media-file-download-cap.test.ts` | Adds regression coverage for oversized streaming and non-streaming downloads |
| Docs | `docs/media.md` | Documents the 512 MB remote podcast/media transcription cap and fail-closed behavior |

## Maintainer impact

- Scope is limited to remote podcast/media transcription download handling.
- Existing small-media and capped in-memory transcription paths are unchanged.
- The patch preserves the current `MAX_REMOTE_MEDIA_BYTES` policy and moves enforcement to the sink where bytes are actually written.
- Oversized remote media now fails earlier and more predictably instead of depending on HEAD support or metadata honesty.

## Fix rationale

- Enforcing the cap during GET streaming handles missing, failed, chunked, or dishonest HEAD responses.
- Checking `nextDownloadedBytes` before each write prevents the temp file from growing past the configured limit.
- Keeping the limit optional on the helper preserves current helper behavior for any trusted internal callers while making the podcast/media remote path secure by default.

## Type of change

- [x] Security fix
- [x] Tests
- [x] Documentation update
- [ ] Refactor with no behavior change

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm exec vitest run tests/security.remote-media-file-download-cap.test.ts`
- [x] `pnpm -s tsx /tmp/summarize-remote-media-cap-proof.ts`
- [x] `pnpm format:check docs/media.md`
- [x] `pnpm exec vitest run tests/transcript.podcast-provider.transcribe-media-url-branches.test.ts tests/security.remote-media-file-download-cap.test.ts`
- [x] `pnpm typecheck`
- [x] `pnpm lint`
- [x] `pnpm test`

Executed successfully locally. `pnpm` emitted the repository's engine warning because the local runtime was Node `v22.22.2` while `package.json` declares `node >=24`; the commands above still completed successfully.

## Disclosure notes

- This PR is bounded to local availability/resource exhaustion through remote media transcription downloads.
- It does not claim remote code execution or credential disclosure.
- I did not find a `SECURITY.md` in the repository, so I am submitting this as a regular hardening PR with a safe regression test.
- No unrelated files are changed.


## Latest follow-up validation

After adding the docs alignment and real HTTP proof, I reran:

- `pnpm exec vitest run tests/security.remote-media-file-download-cap.test.ts`
- `pnpm -s tsx /tmp/summarize-remote-media-cap-proof.ts`
- `pnpm typecheck`
- `pnpm lint`
- `pnpm format:check docs/media.md`

All completed successfully locally. `pnpm` still emitted the repository engine warning because this machine is on Node `v22.22.2` while `package.json` declares `node >=24`.
target.author: hinotoi-agent

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
